subsystem: The `mysql` command-line client (libmysql/client) line-editor. `add_line()` is the routine that ingests one raw input line typed by (or piped to) the interactive `mysql` monitor and folds it into the accumulating statement `buffer` (a MySQL `String`). It performs client-side lexing: it strips leading spaces, honors multi-byte charsets, recognizes backslash client commands (\c, \N, ...), string quoting, single- and multi-line SQL comments, server-side (/*! */) comment markers, and the statement delimiter, dispatching client commands and flushing completed statements to the server.
provenance: Identified from the code shape and identifiers, not an SPDX tag. Strong tells: `COMMANDS *com` / `find_command` / `com->func` / `com->takes_params`, the `String &buffer` type with `.append/.c_ptr/.realloc/.alloced_length/.length`, `DBUG_ENTER/DBUG_RETURN` tracing macros, `charset_info` + `my_isspace/my_ismbchar/use_mb/my_strnncoll` charset helpers, `mysql.server_status & SERVER_STATUS_NO_BACKSLASH_ESCAPES`, `put_info(...,INFO_ERROR)`, `com_go`, `preserve_comments`, `delimiter`/`delimiter_length`, and `HAVE_READLINE`/`add_history`. This is `add_line()` from the MySQL/MariaDB client `client/mysql.cc`. The in-corpus filename `train_0121_cwe863.cpp` and header `primevul_train_2332_vuln` are dataset tags, treated as untrusted and ignored for judgment.
trust_boundary: The `line` argument is fully attacker/user-controllable: it is the raw text of an input line (interactive tty, a piped script, or `mysql < file`). Every byte of `line` — its length, its embedded quotes, backslashes, comment markers, delimiter strings, and multi-byte lead bytes — is hostile input. `charset_info` and the configured `delimiter`/`delimiter_length` are influenced by the same session. The function rewrites `line` IN PLACE (the `out` write cursor advances through the very buffer `pos` reads from) and appends the result to `buffer`, so the security-critical property is that the write cursor never runs past the read cursor and neither runs past the buffer end. External helpers `my_ismbchar`, `is_prefix`, `find_command`, and the `com->func` callbacks sit on the trust boundary because their return values steer pointer arithmetic and buffer lifetime.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Signature and locals. `buffer` is the persistent glob buffer accumulating the current statement across calls; `line` is the newly read raw input line (attacker data); `in_string` is a pointer to a persistent flag/quote-char tracking whether the lexer is currently inside a quoted string (carried across lines, hence a pointer); `ml_comment` is a pointer to the persistent 'inside /* */ multi-line comment' flag; `truncated` says the input line was cut off. Locals: `inchar` holds the current byte; `buff[80]` is a fixed 80-byte scratch buffer used only for an error message; `pos` is the read cursor and `out` is the write cursor — both will point into `line`, giving in-place compaction. `need_space`/`ss_comment` are per-line lexer state. `DBUG_ENTER` is debug tracing.

Early-out: an empty line with an empty buffer is ignored. When built with readline, a non-empty, non-duplicate line is added to command history. `end_of_line` is computed as `line + strlen(line)`, i.e. a pointer to the terminating NUL; it is the only precomputed upper bound for the parse and is used by the multi-byte scanner. Note the `(uint)` cast of `strlen`: on a >4GiB line this would truncate, but interactive/piped lines are not that large in practice.

The main lex loop. `pos` and `out` both start at `line`; each iteration reads `inchar = *pos` and stops at the NUL terminator (loop condition). Because `out` writes into `line` while `pos` reads it, this is an in-place rewrite whose safety rests on `out` never overtaking `pos`. The first block, when comments are not being preserved, drops leading whitespace of a fresh statement (write cursor still at the start and glob buffer empty) by `continue`-ing without emitting the byte.

Multi-byte handling. `my_ismbchar(charset_info, pos, end_of_line)` inspects the bytes at `pos` (bounded, in principle, by `end_of_line`) and returns the byte-length of a valid multi-byte character or 0. If a multi-byte char is present and we are not inside a discarded comment, the `while (length--) *out++ = *pos++;` loop copies all `length` bytes verbatim from the read cursor to the write cursor, then `pos--` compensates for the loop's own `pos++`. In a non-preserved comment, `pos += length - 1` just skips the character without emitting it. Net effect: `pos` and `out` advance by the same amount, keeping the write cursor at or behind the read cursor.

Backslash client-command detection. Outside a multi-line comment, and unless backslash-escapes are disabled inside a string, a `\` may begin a one-character client command. `*++pos` pre-advances the read cursor and fetches the following byte; if that byte is the NUL terminator (a trailing lone backslash) the loop `break`s. If we are inside a string, or the escape is `\N` (NULL literal), the two bytes `\` and the following char are emitted verbatim (`*out++` twice) and the loop continues — here two bytes are written but two were also consumed (`\` and the `*++pos` byte).

If the escaped char names a known client command, the bytes accepted so far are flushed from `line` into `buffer` via `buffer.append(line, out-line)` and the write cursor is reset to the start. The command callback `(*com->func)(&buffer, pos-1)` is invoked; a positive return means Quit. If the command takes parameters, the parser then skips the rest of the argument text: inside a server-side comment it scans forward to the closing `*/`; otherwise it scans to the configured delimiter using `is_prefix(pos+1, delimiter+1)`, landing on the last delimiter byte (`pos += delimiter_length - 1`) or, if none, backing up one. Each scan loop is guarded by `*pos` (stops at NUL), and `*(pos+1)` is only read after `*pos` is confirmed non-NUL.

If the backslash sequence is not a recognized command, an error message is formatted into the fixed `buff[80]` with `sprintf` using the literal format `"Unknown command '\%c'."` and one `%c` argument, then surfaced via `put_info(...,INFO_ERROR)`. The two original bytes (`\` and the unknown char) are then emitted verbatim and lexing continues. The format is a compile-time literal (no user-controlled format string) and the single `%c` expands the output to a fixed ~21 bytes, well within 80.

Delimiter handling: outside comments/strings, if the configured delimiter matches at `pos` (`is_prefix`), the statement is complete. `pos += delimiter_length` steps past the delimiter (safe because `is_prefix` proved those bytes are present). With comment preservation, any whitespace immediately after the delimiter is copied verbatim (`*out++ = *pos++` advances both cursors equally). The accepted bytes are then flushed into `buffer` and `out` is reset.

After the delimiter, an optional trailing single-line comment (`#...` or `-- ...`) is appended whole to `buffer` and `pos` advanced to end. `pos--` compensates for the loop's `pos++`. The now-complete statement text in `buffer` is checked with `find_command`: if it is a client command, its callback runs (`buffer.c_ptr()` passed as arg), otherwise `com_go` sends it to the server. Either way `buffer.length(0)` resets the glob buffer. Note `pos[1]`/`pos[2]` are read only after `*pos=='-'` (non-NUL) is confirmed, and `pos[2]` after `pos[1]=='-'`.

Single-line comment start (`#` or `-- ` at top level). Accepted bytes are flushed; if preserving comments, the rest of the line is appended to `buffer`, and a standalone comment is sent immediately via `com_go`. The loop then `break`s (rest of line is comment). `pos[1]` is only read when `inchar=='-'`, and `pos[2]` only after `pos[1]=='-'`, so these lookaheads never read past the NUL by more than the terminator itself (`!pos[2]` explicitly tolerates end-of-line).

Multi-line comment open `/*` (but not `/*!`). If preserving, both bytes are copied (first with `pos++`, second without, and the loop's `pos++` finishes the advance — 2 read, 2 written). Otherwise `pos++` just skips the `*`. `*ml_comment` is set so subsequent bytes are treated as comment, and accepted bytes are flushed. `*(pos+1)` is guarded by `inchar=='/'`, and `*(pos+2)` by `*(pos+1)=='*'`; short-circuiting prevents reading past the NUL.

Multi-line comment close `*/`. Symmetric to the open: 2 bytes consumed, copied only when preserving. `*ml_comment` is cleared and accepted bytes flushed. Crucially `need_space=1` is set, and the in-code comment states the invariant the whole design relies on: closing `*/` consumes at least 2 bytes but will only add at most 1 byte (the injected space) later, so the write cursor stays at or behind the read cursor and in-place editing is safe.

Default byte handler. It toggles the server-side-comment flag on `/*!` / `*/`, and maintains string state: if the byte equals the currently open quote it closes the string; otherwise, at top level, a `'`, `"`, or backtick opens a string (stored via `*in_string`). Finally, unless inside a discarded comment, the byte is emitted: if `need_space` was set by a just-closed `*/` and the next byte is not itself whitespace, a single space is injected first (`*out++= ' '`). This injected space is the only place the write cursor can gain a byte relative to consumption, and it is paid for by the >=2 bytes the preceding `*/` consumed.

Line finalization. `length = out - line` is the count of rewritten bytes. Unless the line is a truncated `DELIMITER` command (detected by comparing the first 9 bytes against the literal "delimiter" via `my_strnncoll`, but only when `length >= 9` thanks to short-circuiting) or ends inside a string/comment, a newline is appended in place (`*out++='\n'`). The glob `buffer` is grown with `realloc` if `buffer.length()+length` would exceed the allocation, then the rewritten `line[0..length)` is appended to `buffer`. A truthy `buffer.append` return signals error/Quit. The `String` object owns the growth of `buffer`; only `line` is edited in place.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The whole routine rewrites `line` in place: `out` (write cursor) advances through the same buffer that `pos` (read cursor) reads. Safety depends on the global invariant out <= pos at every `*out++`, so that the compacted output never runs past the terminating NUL or the caller's `line` allocation (out-of-bounds write, CWE-787). The invariant is maintained by a scattered set of local accounting arguments (each `*out++` in the loop is paired with an equal-or-greater `pos` advance, and the one asymmetric write — the injected space — is 'pre-paid' by a `*/` that consumed two bytes). This is fragile: any future code path that emits more bytes than it consumes, or any helper (`my_ismbchar`, `is_prefix`) that misreports a length, silently breaks it and corrupts memory. An implementation reviewer must prove out<=pos holds on every branch.

The multi-byte copy blindly trusts `length = my_ismbchar(charset_info, pos, end_of_line)`: it copies exactly `length` bytes from `pos` to `out`. `my_ismbchar` is passed `end_of_line` precisely so it will not classify a truncated trailing multi-byte sequence as a full character; correctness therefore rests entirely on that helper honoring the end pointer for every configured charset. If for any charset `my_ismbchar` can return a length that reaches beyond the last real byte (e.g. a lead byte at end-of-line for which the follower-count is returned without bounds-checking), `*pos++` over-reads past the NUL (CWE-125) and the mirrored `*out++` over-writes the `line` buffer (CWE-787). The invariant to confirm: my_ismbchar never returns a length exceeding `end_of_line - pos`.

This is the single spot where the write cursor can outrun consumption: when `need_space` is set, TWO bytes (' ' then `inchar`) are written while only one byte (`inchar`) is consumed this iteration. The design comment at lines 222-224 asserts this is safe because `need_space` is only set right after a `*/` close that consumed >=2 bytes without emitting them. That reasoning holds only if `need_space` cannot survive to an iteration whose preceding consumption was less than the extra byte owed — e.g. if a future edit set `need_space` on a path that consumed a single byte, or if `need_space` persists across a `continue` that already emitted output. If that accounting is ever violated, `out` passes `pos` and the in-place rewrite overflows `line` (CWE-787). Confirm every producer of `need_space=1` is preceded by a >=2-byte non-emitting consume.

After the loop, a newline is written in place at `line[out-line]`. At loop exit `pos == end_of_line` and out<=pos, so in the worst case (a line copied 1:1 with no compaction) `out == end_of_line`, and this write lands on the byte that held the terminating NUL. That byte is inside the caller's allocation only if `line` was sized to hold the string plus its NUL (the readline/getline contract) — which it normally is — so this is at the very edge of the buffer. The risk is latent: if a caller ever passes a `line` buffer exactly `strlen(line)` bytes with no slack for the NUL/newline, this becomes a one-byte out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). Confirm `line`'s backing allocation always has at least one byte of headroom beyond `strlen(line)`.

Command dispatch invokes an indirect callback `com->func` (again at line 140) with `&buffer` mid-parse. These client-command callbacks can mutate, reset, or reallocate the `String buffer` object while `add_line` still holds `line`/`pos`/`out` (which point into a different buffer, so those stay valid) but continues to use `buffer` afterward — e.g. line 148 `buffer.length(0)` and the finalization block operate on `buffer` after the callback returned non-fatally. If any callback frees or invalidates the `buffer` backing store and returns <= 0 (non-quit), subsequent use is a use-after-free / stale-pointer hazard (CWE-416). The contract to confirm: every `com->func` either fully quits (returns >0) or leaves `buffer` in a valid, owned state.

`sprintf` into the fixed 80-byte `buff` is the classic unbounded-copy pattern (CWE-120). Here it is SAFE: the format is a compile-time string literal (so no CWE-134 format-string exposure from user data) and its only conversion is a single `%c`, giving a fixed output of about 21 bytes — far under 80. The note exists so the reviewer confirms the format never gains a `%s`/width from `inchar` and `buff` is not later assumed NUL-bounded beyond 80; it is not an exploitable overflow as written.

summary: This is the MySQL client's in-place line lexer `add_line()`, which rewrites an attacker-controlled input line into an accumulating statement buffer while handling multi-byte charsets, backslash commands, quotes, comments, and delimiters. There is no slam-dunk overflow in the shown code; the dominant risk is that the whole design writes into the same buffer it reads and relies on a scattered out<=pos invariant plus the correctness of external helpers (my_ismbchar bounding to end_of_line, the >=2-byte-consume backing every injected space). The realistic latent exposures are an out-of-bounds write if any branch (notably the multi-byte copy or the need_space path) ever emits more than it consumed, and a callback-lifetime hazard on the com->func dispatches; the sprintf is a benign fixed-format use.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed buffer written by sprintf at line 100; enumerated and shown safe (literal format, bounded %c).
In-place rewrite; whole-function out<=pos invariant is the central OOB-write contract.
Upper bound used only by my_ismbchar; the (uint) cast is not attackable for realistic line sizes.
Copies my_ismbchar's length verbatim; OOB read/write if that length exceeds end_of_line - pos.
Skips a multi-byte char without writing; length came from a bounded my_ismbchar call, no buffer write.
NUL-terminated: a trailing lone backslash reads the NUL and breaks; no over-read.
Indirect callbacks can alter/free `buffer` mid-parse; lifetime/UAF contract.
Loop guarded by *pos != 0; *(pos+1) read only when *pos=='*', short-circuit-safe.
is_prefix bounds the match; loop stops at NUL; advance lands on last matched delimiter byte.
Fixed-buffer sprintf pattern; recorded as low/benign (literal format, bounded output).
Only reached after is_prefix(pos, delimiter) proved delimiter_length bytes present; lands at most on NUL.
Advances both cursors equally and stops at first non-space/NUL; preserves out<=pos.
Appends into the self-growing String; pos advanced to NUL; no fixed-buffer write.
Reads gated by preceding non-NUL byte checks and short-circuit; `!pos[2]` explicitly tolerates end-of-line.
Short-circuit lookahead; when preserving, 2 bytes consumed and 2 written, out<=pos maintained.
Consumes >=2 bytes; the injected space it authorizes is covered by that consumption.
Lookahead guarded by inchar and *(pos+1) checks with short-circuit; no over-read past NUL.
Pure state flag update on a single byte; carries no buffer-length contract.
Only asymmetric write (2 out for 1 consumed); the out<=pos invariant hinges on its pairing with a prior *//* consume.
In-place newline write can land on the NUL slot; relies on caller allocating headroom beyond strlen.
Guarded by `length < 9 ||` short-circuit, so the 9-byte compare runs only when >=9 bytes exist.
String object manages its own growth; append uses explicit length, not NUL scanning.

findings.title: In-place line rewrite relies on an unenforced out<=pos invariant (OOB write surface)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write via in-place compaction (broken read/write-cursor invariant)
findings.invariant: At every `*out++` in the loop and at the trailing newline write, the write cursor must satisfy out <= pos <= end_of_line, so the compacted output never exceeds the bytes read from `line` nor the caller's allocation.
findings.failure_mode: The function writes into the very buffer it reads. The invariant is upheld only by scattered per-branch accounting (each emit paired with an equal-or-greater read; the single injected space pre-paid by a two-byte `*/` consume). Any branch that emits more than it consumes, or any helper that misreports a length, lets `out` pass `pos` and overwrite past the NUL / end of `line`, corrupting adjacent memory. Trigger is crafted input exercising the offending branch; impact is memory corruption in the client process.
findings.what_to_confirm: Enumerate every `*out++` site and prove the bytes written on that path are <= the bytes consumed since the last flush, and that `line`'s allocation always exceeds the final `length`.

findings.title: Multi-byte copy trusts my_ismbchar length without re-bounding to end_of_line
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read and mirrored out-of-bounds write on untrusted multi-byte length
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (my_ismbchar in charset library, file not provided)
findings.invariant: my_ismbchar must never return a length greater than (end_of_line - pos) for any configured charset, so the copy loop cannot read or write past the last valid byte.
findings.failure_mode: If a charset's mb classifier returns a full-character length for a truncated trailing sequence (ignoring the supplied end pointer), the `while (length--)` loop reads past the terminating NUL (over-read, CWE-125) and simultaneously writes those bytes via `*out++` past the compacted region (over-write, CWE-787). Attacker supplies a line ending in a lead byte for such a charset. Impact: over-read/over-write in the client.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify my_ismbchar for every supported charset honors `end_of_line` and returns 0 (not a positive length) for a lead byte lacking its full follower set at end-of-buffer.

findings.title: need_space injects an extra byte, the only write not backed by consumption
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write if the space-injection accounting is violated
findings.invariant: `need_space=1` must only ever be set on a path that already consumed at least two bytes without emitting them (the `*/` comment close at lines 207-224), so the later two-writes-for-one-consume here still keeps out <= pos.
findings.failure_mode: As written the invariant holds (the sole `need_space=1` is the `*/` branch, per its own comment at lines 222-224). It is called out because it is the single asymmetric write in the loop: if a future edit sets `need_space` after a one-byte consume, or lets it persist across a path that already used its slack, this branch pushes `out` past `pos` and overflows `line`. Trigger would be that hypothetical mis-set state; impact is a one-byte-plus in-place overflow.
findings.what_to_confirm: Grep every assignment of `need_space=1` and confirm each is immediately preceded by a two-or-more-byte non-emitting consume before the next `*out++`.

findings.title: Trailing in-place newline write lands on the terminating-NUL slot
findings.bug_class: Edge-of-buffer out-of-bounds write dependent on caller allocation slack
findings.invariant: The buffer backing `line` must be allocated with at least one byte beyond strlen(line) (room for the original NUL / this newline), which the readline/getline input path normally guarantees.
findings.failure_mode: In the worst case (a line copied 1:1 with no compaction) `out == end_of_line` here, so `*out++='\n'` writes at the position that held the terminating NUL. That is within a properly sized input buffer, but if any caller ever passes a `line` sized exactly strlen(line) with no NUL headroom, this is a one-byte out-of-bounds write. Impact: single-byte overflow past `line`.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm all callers allocate `line` from an input routine that reserves space for the NUL terminator (so writing at end_of_line stays in-bounds).

findings.title: Command callbacks (com->func) may invalidate `buffer` mid-parse
findings.bug_class: Use-after-free / stale reference on callback-mutated buffer object
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (command handler table com->func, file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every `com->func` client-command handler must, on any non-quit (<=0) return, leave the `String buffer` in a valid, still-owned state; it must not free or leave dangling the storage that `add_line` continues to use (lines 148, 268-271).
findings.failure_mode: If a handler that returns <=0 releases or reallocates `buffer`'s backing store, the subsequent `buffer.length(0)` / `buffer.realloc` / `buffer.append` operate on freed or stale memory. Trigger is any input that dispatches such a handler; impact is a use-after-free in the client. This is latent and depends on the handler table not shown here.
findings.what_to_confirm: Audit each function in the COMMANDS table for whether it can free/replace the passed String and return a non-positive value while add_line keeps using `buffer`.

audit_checklist: Prove the out <= pos invariant on EVERY branch that does `*out++`, so the in-place rewrite of `line` can never write past what it read or past the buffer end.
Confirm my_ismbchar honors `end_of_line` for all configured charsets and returns 0 for a truncated trailing multi-byte sequence, so `while (length--) *out++ = *pos++` cannot over-read/over-write.
Verify the ONLY setter of need_space is the `*/` close (lines 207-224) that consumed >=2 bytes, so the injected space at line 241 never pushes out past pos.
Check that `line`'s backing allocation always has at least one byte of slack beyond strlen(line), so `*out++='\n'` at line 265 stays in-bounds.
Audit every COMMANDS `com->func` handler (lines 70, 140) to ensure it cannot free/realloc/invalidate `buffer` and then return <=0 while add_line keeps using it.
Confirm the `sprintf(buff, ...)` format at line 100 stays a literal with a single bounded `%c`, keeping output well under 80 bytes.

Re-check every `*(pos+1)` / `pos[1]` / `*(pos+2)` / `pos[2]` lookahead (lines 81, 89, 127-128, 151-159, 190-191, 228-231) is short-circuited behind a confirmed non-NUL predecessor so none reads past the terminator.
Validate that `pos += delimiter_length`, `pos += delimiter_length - 1`, and `pos += strlen(pos)` never advance `pos` beyond end_of_line before the next dereference.
